data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a 'Schumpeterian' wave of creative destruction driven by a military-social revolution from the margins: mobile infantry warbands armed with innovative javelins and Naue II swords, recruited from the semi-nomadic and lower-class groups excluded from palatial wealth, systematically dismantled the brittle, chariot-based palace elites, only to fail to establish stable successor states, leading to a 'dark age'.", "mechanism": "The palatial system's military power rested on an elite chariot corps, which was tactically and logistically inflexible. The diffusion of cheap, effective infantry weapons (javelins, long swords) and 'swarm' tactics allowed large numbers of non-elite warriors to overwhelm chariot formations on broken terrain. These warbands, motivated by plunder and social mobility, targeted the undefended or lightly-defended administrative and storage centers of the palaces. The destruction was not a single invasion but a contagious wave of raiding and rebellion, as success attracted more followers. The system collapsed because the palaces could not adapt their military doctrine or social structure quickly enough to counter this new threat. Egypt survived under Ramesses III by using its centralized state to hire mercenaries and fight a massive, set-piece defensive battle in the Delta, a geographic and organizational option not available to the smaller, decentralized palace states.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria (which adapted and survived)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Destruction layers across all sites are consistently dated centuries apart, showing no synchronicity.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The Naue II sword and javelin types are found exclusively in elite warrior graves, not in lower-class or mercenary contexts.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian records describe the Sea Peoples as a single, unified ethnic army with a royal leader, not as a diverse swarm of warbands.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This refines the military-technological cascade from artifact 79d71b90 by adding the crucial social dimension of marginalized groups (as in 7dbbd147) and the 'Schumpeterian' framing. It explains the synchronicity through a contagious, imitative process of raiding. The mechanism for Egypt's survival is specific: a centralized defensive battle in the Delta, contrasting with the fragmented palace defenses."}
